lens.included_activities: Independent group and multisite optometry practices in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly provide eye examinations, refraction, disease detection and management, corrective-lens prescribing and fitting, contact-lens care, vision therapy, pre- and postoperative care, and integrated optical dispensing to external patients and payers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive hospital, HMO, or government units; ophthalmology practices; pure optical-goods retailers without optometric practice; online eyewear or software businesses; shell or non-operating entities; non-control financing positions; and solo or owner-dependent practices that cannot support a transferable operating company or fall below the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive the service and may pay directly, while medical or vision insurers fund covered examinations and procedures. Practices may also earn product revenue from eyeglasses, contact lenses, and related optical goods. Clinical revenue is generally visit- or procedure-based, whereas optical revenue combines product margin and fitting service; repeat examinations, disease monitoring, and prescription renewals create repeat demand without guaranteed contracts.

a.bridge: O*NET's national Optometrists task map, a multicenter ambient-scribe study of physicians and advanced-practice practitioners, and CMS recognition of AI-interpreted retinal imaging are translated into the wage-weighted exposed share of a scaled optometry office. The estimate includes documentation, intake, scheduling, billing support, patient messaging, image pre-analysis, test summarization, inventory and ordering, recall, and portions of optical sales support; it reserves patient examination, complex interpretation, treatment choice, prescribing, fitting, procedures, counseling, exceptions, and accountability for people. The clinician-only O*NET map may understate support and retail labor, while physician health-system documentation results may overstate EHR burden and integration readiness; CMS's existing retinal-imaging code demonstrates that some diagnostic automation is already deployed and therefore must not be counted as wholly new opportunity.
a.rationale: Optometry combines digitally captured test data and standardized administrative and retail workflows with high-contact licensed care. O*NET shows core work in instrument-based examination, test analysis, prescribing, treatment planning, counseling, and referral, plus daily face-to-face interaction and high accuracy requirements. AI can assist the information-processing perimeter, but the wage-heavy clinician core and hands-on examination and fitting place a ceiling on substitution.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion and payroll study of independent LMM optometry groups separating optometrists, technicians, opticians, billing, front desk, call center, inventory, and management work, with before/after AI deployment results and current automation baselines, would materially move this range.
caveats: O*NET measures optometrist duties, not the full occupation and wage mix of NAICS 621320 offices.
Ambient-scribe results come from physicians and advanced-practice practitioners rather than optometrists.
Automated instruments and retinal interpretation are already present in some practices, so gross technical capability overstates the not-yet-automated share.

rho.bridge: The AMA's 2026 physician survey supplies a U.S. clinical-adoption proxy, the six-system ambulatory study demonstrates implemented documentation assistance, and CMS's automated retinal-imaging code supplies an optometry-adjacent production foothold. The AMA reports 72% of physicians incorporating at least one surveyed AI use case in 2026, while CMS describes point-of-care AI interpretation of a retinal examination without requiring ophthalmologist interpretation as a diagnostic service. Physicians and health systems have greater integration resources than many independent optometry groups, tending to overstate adoption, but optometry's structured imaging, testing, EHR, scheduling, and inventory systems may speed packaged deployment over five years.
rho.rationale: Implementation can be meaningful because several exposed workflows are already digital and diagnostic AI has a reimbursable precedent. Realization remains constrained by device and EHR integration, patient privacy, vendor cost, state scope rules, diagnostic error, cybersecurity, clinician review, workflow redesign, staff training, and the need to distinguish automated screening from comprehensive eye care.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of independent U.S. optometry groups by size measuring paid AI deployment, diagnostic-device penetration, EHR integration, override and referral rates, documentation edits, staff hours, optical conversion, and realized payroll changes would materially tighten this estimate.
caveats: The AMA adoption population is physicians rather than optometrists.
The ambient-scribe study was voluntary and short-duration, and its health-system support may not transfer to independent groups.
CMS code 92229 covers a narrow retinal-imaging use case rather than broad autonomous eye care.

e.rationale: NAICS 621320 is defined around independent optometric practice and explicitly includes clinical care plus prescription eyewear and contact-lens provision, so most LMM firms serve external patients repeatedly. Eligibility is reduced for captive offices in hospitals or HMOs, government units, optical-dominant operations with weak clinical identity, non-control situations, and practices whose patient or referral goodwill and licensed production cannot transfer beyond the selling owner. The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms around the EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 621320 companies in the normalized EBITDA band showing ownership, locations, optometrist and optician staffing, clinical versus optical revenue, payer mix, owner production, repeat-patient behavior, and institutional affiliation would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public source measures the share of frozen LMM firms satisfying every lens criterion.
BLS occupation shares do not map cleanly to enterprise ownership or eligibility.
The dataset's LMM count uses a broad healthcare-provider margin rather than observed optometry EBITDA.

s5.rationale: BizBuySell explicitly includes optometry in its sold medical-practice benchmark population, establishing a transfer channel. Five-year qualifying availability still depends on owner timing, licensed buyer or employed-clinician capacity, payer credentialing, lease and equipment obligations, patient retention, optical inventory, and the separation of goodwill from the selling doctor. The frozen LMM population is larger than the chiropractic counterpart but remains estimate-based, and public transaction data provide no denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort from optometry practice brokers, lenders, state boards, vision-plan networks, or transaction databases identifying eligible LMM practices, completed control transfers, failed listings, recapitalizations, seller transitions, and post-close patient and clinician retention would materially move this estimate.
caveats: BizBuySell provides medical-practice benchmarks but no optometry-only sale probability or eligible-firm denominator.
The platform's median sold practice is smaller than the target EBITDA band.
A transfer can fail economically if patients, payer panels, or optical sales follow the departing owner.

q.rationale: Visit- and procedure-based clinical reimbursement can let a practice retain administrative and throughput gains without repricing each labor hour. Optical products add a different mechanism: automation can reduce ordering, inventory, sales-support, and remakes, but prescription portability lets patients shop elsewhere and exposes eyewear margins to online and retail competition. Managed vision plans, payer fee schedules, procurement, vendor fees, and customer expectations can claim part of the benefit.
q.change_evidence: Practice-level contracts and ledgers showing clinical reimbursement, optical gross margin, managed-vision discounts, prescription capture, repricing, staff cost, remakes, inventory turns, and contribution margin before and after AI deployment would materially change the range.
caveats: Public evidence does not quantify the lens's clinical-versus-optical revenue or gross-benefit mix.
The FTC rule documents prescription portability and competition but not the magnitude of AI savings passed through to patients.
Freed clinician or technician capacity has little value when appointment demand is the binding constraint.

d5.bridge: BLS projects optometrist employment to grow 8% from 2024 to 2034. Taking the square root of 1.08 yields an approximately 1.04 five-year directional demand multiplier. Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 621320 and includes optometrists working in physician offices and outpatient centers; it may overstate office demand if productivity or non-office employment grows faster, or understate service quantity if automated testing increases visits per clinician.
d5.rationale: BLS identifies aging, refractive errors, digital eye strain, and diabetes-related retinal disease as continuing sources of eye-care demand. Downside comes from retail and online substitution, autonomous screening, payer limits, price competition, and greater productivity from diagnostic equipment. The range therefore allows modest contraction as well as growth around the occupational projection.
d5.change_evidence: A 621320-specific constant-dollar revenue and encounter forecast segmented by clinical exams, medical eye care, contact lenses, eyewear, payer, age, disease state, online substitution, and automated screening would materially move this estimate.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation rather than real output or visit quantity for offices of optometrists.
The occupation includes work settings outside NAICS 621320.
Product sales, clinical services, and screening can follow different demand paths.

o.rationale: Comprehensive eye examinations, prescribing, disease management, treatment planning, procedures, counseling, referral, and fitting continue to require an accountable licensed optometrist or trained staff under supervision, and every state requires optometrist licensure. However, CMS already recognizes AI interpretation for a narrow retinal-imaging service, while federal prescription-release rules allow patients to separate the examination from eyewear purchase. Screening, refraction support, routine monitoring, and optical purchasing can therefore migrate toward automated or self-service channels even though complex care remains operator-led.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous screening and refraction penetration, comprehensive-exam substitution, state-law changes, false-negative and referral rates, patient willingness to use remote or retail channels, optical e-commerce share, and human escalation frequency would materially change this range.
caveats: The estimate distinguishes continued need for an accountable practice from headcount per encounter.
State scope-of-practice rules differ even though licensure is universal.
The automated retinal-imaging precedent covers one disease-screening task and still routes positive or inconclusive cases to eye-care professionals.

narrative.executive_view: Scaled optometry combines repeat licensed clinical care with structured diagnostic data, administration, and optical retail, creating a broader AI-assistance surface than many hands-on health practices. AI can improve documentation, image triage, scheduling, billing, inventory, recall, and sales support, but comprehensive examination, prescribing, disease management, fitting, and accountability remain human-led. The strongest operating form is a multi-doctor group whose payer access, brand, protocols, staff, and patient relationships survive the founder.
narrative.ai_impact: Ambient documentation, test summarization, image pre-analysis, coding support, patient messaging, appointment optimization, inventory forecasting, and optical-order workflows can reduce clerical burden and avoid some hiring. Existing AI-interpreted retinal imaging demonstrates real diagnostic automation, but its narrow scope also shows the limit: comprehensive eye health, treatment choice, prescribing, exceptions, and patient communication still require licensed judgment and review.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed clinical fees can preserve part of productivity gains, while optical operations can benefit from lower administrative cost, better inventory, and fewer errors. Capture is weakened by managed-vision pricing, vendor costs, retail competition, and federal prescription portability that lets patients buy eyewear elsewhere. The benefit depends on converting saved time into more care or lower staffing needs rather than idle capacity.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen LMM count is estimate-based but larger than many clinician-office categories. Medical-practice sales evidence explicitly includes optometry yet does not reveal an optometry-specific transaction rate. Actual control availability depends on owner timing, licensed clinical continuity, payer credentialing, patient retention, leases, equipment, inventory, and whether goodwill is institutional rather than personal.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects optometrist employment growth, with aging, myopia and other refractive errors, digital eye strain, and diabetes supporting demand. Automation and retail or online substitution can shift where routine screening and eyewear are supplied, while disease management and complex eye care remain durable. Clinical services and optical product demand should not be assumed to move together.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are the true staff and revenue mix, the margin-bridged firm count, lack of a transaction denominator, uneven AI and device adoption, diagnostic liability, privacy and cybersecurity, payer reimbursement, prescription leakage, online competition, and founder dependence. Existing automation can reduce the incremental opportunity, while overreliance on narrow screening AI could harm quality or referral capture.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured diagnostic, administrative, and optical workflows offer multiple AI-enabled efficiency levers around a durable recurring need for licensed eye care.
narrative.principal_weakness: Prescription portability, retail and online competition, and partial automation can separate optical economics and routine screening from the practice while licensed-clinician continuity still constrains transfers.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 621320 Offices of Optometrists
sources.supports: Defines 621320 as independent O.D. practices examining, diagnosing, treating, and managing visual-system disease and providing corrective lenses, vision therapy, and optician-like fitting and sales.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Optometrists (29-1041.00)
sources.supports: Provides the optometrist task and work-context map, including instrument-based eye examination, test analysis, treatment planning, prescribing, fitting, counseling, referral, daily face-to-face discussion, frequent contact, and high accuracy requirements.

sources.title: U.S. BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Optometrists
sources.supports: Reports 47,800 optometrist jobs in 2024, work-setting shares including 54% in optometrist offices and 11% self-employed, universal state licensure, 8% projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and aging, refractive errors, digital eye strain, and diabetes as demand drivers.

sources.title: American Medical Association: 2026 Physician AI Survey
sources.supports: Reports U.S. physician AI use, including 72% incorporating at least one surveyed use case in 2026, and identifies privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, training, and oversight as adoption considerations.

sources.title: JAMA Network Open: Use of Ambient AI Scribes to Reduce Administrative Burden and Professional Burnout
sources.supports: A 2024 pre/post quality-improvement study of 263 ambulatory clinicians across six U.S. health systems found lower documentation burden and an average-equivalent 10.8 minutes saved per workday after 30 days of ambient-scribe use, with voluntary participation and no optometry-specific sample.

sources.title: CMS MLN Matters MM12071: Remote Retinal Imaging CPT 92229
sources.supports: Describes CPT 92229 for point-of-care automated retinal-image analysis using AI to interpret the eye examination without requiring ophthalmologist interpretation as a diagnostic service, demonstrating a narrow operational automation precedent.

sources.title: Federal Trade Commission: Final Eyeglass Rule Updates
sources.supports: States that optometrists and ophthalmologists must provide a free prescription copy immediately after a refractive exam and before offering eyeglasses, enabling consumers to shop elsewhere and supporting optical-retail competition and compliance burden.

sources.title: BizBuySell: Medical Practice Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reports transaction and valuation benchmarks for sold medical practices from 2021 through 2025 and explicitly identifies optometry among included specialties, demonstrating a transfer channel while not providing an optometry-specific probability or eligible-firm denominator.
